description: Rules governing playing together: communication constraints, systemic opposition, team structures, and mutual assistance.

communication.description: What players may say, show, and signal to each other.

focuses.limited_table_talk: Discussion is allowed but specific topics, such as the exact contents of your hand, are forbidden (The Crew's restrictions on discussing your cards).
focuses.no_communication_rules: Players may not talk or signal at all, relying on shared conventions and timing (The Mind).
focuses.coded_communication: Information flows only through a constrained formal channel with a fixed grammar (Hanabi's rationed color-and-number clues).
focuses.unrestricted_discussion_quarterbacking: Fully open planning is allowed, which invites a dominant player to direct everyone; some designs deliberately counter this alpha-player problem (Pandemic).
focuses.inverted_hand_visibility: Players see others' hidden information but not their own (Hanabi's outward-facing hands).
focuses.sanctioned_nonverbal_signals: Specific gestures or physical signals are the only sanctioned way to communicate (Magic Maze's 'do something!' pawn).
focuses.timed_communication_windows: Talking is permitted only during designated windows and cut off elsewhere (the communication blackouts announced by Space Alert's soundtrack).
focuses.dedicated_clue_giver_roles: One player knows the answer and may communicate only through a tightly constrained clue channel (the spymaster in Codenames, the ghost in Mysterium).

challenge.description: How the game system itself provides opposition and difficulty.

focuses.scaling_difficulty: Difficulty automatically adjusts to player count or progress so the challenge stays proportionate (per-player-count adjustments in Spirit Island and Pandemic).
focuses.selectable_difficulty_modules: Players opt into difficulty knobs such as adversaries, scenarios, or harsher decks (Spirit Island's leveled adversaries).
focuses.automa_opponents: A card-driven bot simulates a competing player for solo or low-count play (the Automa decks of Scythe and Viticulture).
focuses.deterministic_ai_flowcharts: Enemies follow published if-then decision rules that players can learn and play around (monster focus rules in Gloomhaven, solo-bot flowcharts).
focuses.boss_ai_decks: A boss or monster acts through its own shuffled behavior deck, making it dangerous but variable (the AI cards of Kingdom Death: Monster).
focuses.event_deck_pressure: A recurring event deck injects escalating problems the team must absorb each round (the event deck in Robinson Crusoe).
focuses.doom_track_escalation: A visible track advances toward defeat, compressing the time available to win (Pandemic's outbreak track).
focuses.enemy_spawn_rules: Systemic rules spawn and reinforce enemies at defined times and places (zombie spawn zones in Zombicide).
focuses.staged_escalation_acts: The scenario advances through scripted stages that change the rules and raise the stakes (agenda and act decks in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).

structure.description: How teams and sides are organized.

focuses.fully_cooperative: All players form one team that wins or loses together against the game system (Spirit Island, Hanabi).
focuses.one_vs_many: One empowered player opposes a coordinated team of the rest (the overlord in Descent).
focuses.semi_coop_selfish_goals: Players must cooperate to survive but hold individual goals, and only some of them can win (secret objectives in Dead of Winter).
focuses.team_vs_team: Fixed teams compete against each other while cooperating internally (Captain Sonar's dueling submarine crews, bridge partnerships).
focuses.formal_alliance_rules: Explicit rules let players form, benefit from, and break alliances, sometimes sharing victory (alliances in Dune).
focuses.coop_competitive_phase_shifts: Play alternates between cooperative and competitive modes within a single game (Cutthroat Caverns' cooperate-then-claim-the-kill encounters).
focuses.asymmetric_team_roles: Teammates wield distinct roles with unique powers, so coordination means combining complementary strengths (Pandemic's role cards).
focuses.solo_mode_design: Official rules support one player against the system through dedicated variants or scenarios (Mage Knight's solo play).

assistance.description: Mechanics through which players directly help one another.

focuses.shared_resources: The team draws from common stockpiles rather than private supplies (the shared resource pool of Robinson Crusoe).
focuses.restricted_teammate_trading: Players may pass cards or resources to teammates only under tight positional or timing limits (Pandemic's Share Knowledge rules).
focuses.trading_actions_between_players: Players can spend their own actions or capacity to let teammates act or grow (Spirit Island's gift powers that hand energy and elements to another spirit).
focuses.rescue_mechanics: Dedicated actions recover captured, lost, or endangered characters (carrying victims to safety in Flash Point: Fire Rescue).
focuses.downed_player_rules: Players reduced to zero enter an incapacitated state with limited options instead of leaving the game (incapacitated heroes in Sentinels of the Multiverse, death saves in D&D).
focuses.jointly_controlled_pieces: Players share control of the same pieces, with each player empowered to do different things with them (Magic Maze's communally moved pawns).
focuses.teammate_buff_support: Abilities exist chiefly to enhance other players' actions, defenses, or rolls (bardic inspiration in D&D).
focuses.damage_interposition_tanking: Players can redirect incoming harm from allies onto themselves (taunt and bodyguard abilities in cooperative dungeon crawlers).
focuses.collective_health_pool: The team shares a single life total whose depletion loses the game for everyone (Gravehold's shared life in Aeon's End).
